question: 4. Show that the coefficients \( a_{1}, a_{2}, a_{3}, \ldots \) in the expansion \( \frac{1}{4}[1+x- \) \( \left.\left(1-6 x+x^{2}\right)^{12}\right]=a_{1} x+a_{2} x^{2}+a_{3} x^{3}+\cdots \) are positive integers.
solution:
Solution. Let
\[
y=y(x)=\frac{1}{4}\left[1+x-\left(1-6 x+x^{2}\right)^{1 / 2}\right]
\]

By the general binomial theorem \( \left(1-6 x+x^{2}\right)^{1 / 2} \), and hence \( y \), can be expanded in a power series convergent for values of \( x \) such that \( \left|x^{2}\right|+|6 x| \) \( <1 \). Since \( y(0)=0 \), the series has the form
\[
y(x)=a_{1} x+a_{2} x^{2}+a_{3} x^{3}+\cdots
\]

Now
\[
2 y^{2}-(1+x) y+x=0
\]
so if we substitute the power series for \( y \) in (2) we have
\[
2\left(a_{1} x+a_{2} x^{2}+a_{3} x^{3}+\cdots\right)^{2}=(1+x)\left(a_{1} x+a_{2} x^{2}+a_{3} x^{3}+\cdots\right)-x
\]

Comparing coefficients of \( x \), we see that \( a_{1}=1 \), and for \( n>1 \),
\[
2\left(a_{1} a_{n-1}+a_{2} a_{n-2}+\cdots+a_{n-1} a_{1}\right)=a_{n}+a_{n-1}
\]

Hence \( a_{2}=1 \) and
\[
a_{n}=3 a_{n-1}+2 \sum_{i=2}^{n-2} a_{i} a_{n-i} \text { for } n>2
\]

Therefore, if \( a_{1}, a_{2}, \ldots, a_{n-1} \) are positive integers, \( a_{n} \) is also a positive integer. Since \( a_{1} \) and \( a_{2} \) are positive integers, all the coefficients \( \left\{a_{i}\right\} \) are positive integers.

descriptive_long.question: 4. Show that the coefficients \( coeffone, coefftwo, coeffthr, \ldots \) in the expansion \( \frac{1}{4}\left[1+inputvar-\left(1-6 inputvar+inputvar^{2}\right)^{12}\right]=coeffone inputvar+coefftwo inputvar^{2}+coeffthr inputvar^{3}+\cdots \) are positive integers.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Let
\[
outputval=outputval(inputvar)=\frac{1}{4}\left[1+inputvar-\left(1-6 inputvar+inputvar^{2}\right)^{1 / 2}\right]
\]

By the general binomial theorem \( \left(1-6 inputvar+inputvar^{2}\right)^{1 / 2} \), and hence \( outputval \), can be expanded in a power series convergent for values of \( inputvar \) such that \( \left|inputvar^{2}\right|+|6 inputvar|<1 \). Since \( outputval(0)=0 \), the series has the form
\[
outputval(inputvar)=coeffone inputvar+coefftwo inputvar^{2}+coeffthr inputvar^{3}+\cdots
\]

Now
\[
2\,outputval^{2}-(1+inputvar)\,outputval+inputvar=0
\]
so if we substitute the power series for \( outputval \) we have
\[
2\left(coeffone inputvar+coefftwo inputvar^{2}+coeffthr inputvar^{3}+\cdots\right)^{2}=(1+inputvar)\left(coeffone inputvar+coefftwo inputvar^{2}+coeffthr inputvar^{3}+\cdots\right)-inputvar .
\]

Comparing coefficients of \( inputvar \) gives \( coeffone=1 \), and for \( indexer>1 \),
\[
2\left(coeffone\,coeffnmin+coefftwo\,coeffnmin2+\cdots+coeffnmin\,coeffone\right)=coeffsubn+coeffnmin .
\]

Hence \( coefftwo=1 \) and
\[
coeffsubn=3\,coeffnmin+2\sum_{i=2}^{indexer-2} coeffsubi\,coeffnmini \quad\text{for } indexer>2 .
\]

Therefore, if \( coeffone, coefftwo, \ldots, coeffnmin \) are positive integers, then \( coeffsubn \) is also a positive integer. Since \( coeffone \) and \( coefftwo \) are positive integers, all the coefficients \( \{coeffsubi\} \) are positive integers.

descriptive_long_confusing.question: 4. Show that the coefficients \( turnpike, footbridge, workbench, \ldots \) in the expansion \( \frac{1}{4}[1+cloudless- \) \( \left.\left(1-6 cloudless+cloudless^{2}\right)^{12}\right]=turnpike\, cloudless+footbridge\, cloudless^{2}+workbench\, cloudless^{3}+\cdots \) are positive integers.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Let
\[
marshland = marshland(cloudless)=\frac{1}{4}\left[1+cloudless-\left(1-6 cloudless+cloudless^{2}\right)^{1 / 2}\right]
\]

By the general binomial theorem \( \left(1-6 cloudless+cloudless^{2}\right)^{1 / 2} \), and hence \( marshland \), can be expanded in a power series convergent for values of \( cloudless \) such that \( \left|cloudless^{2}\right|+|6 cloudless| <1 \). Since \( marshland(0)=0 \), the series has the form
\[
marshland(cloudless)=turnpike\, cloudless+footbridge\, cloudless^{2}+workbench\, cloudless^{3}+\cdots
\]

Now
\[
2\, marshland^{2}-(1+cloudless)\, marshland+cloudless=0
\]
so if we substitute the power series for \( marshland \) in (2) we have
\[
2\left(turnpike\, cloudless+footbridge\, cloudless^{2}+workbench\, cloudless^{3}+\cdots\right)^{2}=(1+cloudless)\left(turnpike\, cloudless+footbridge\, cloudless^{2}+workbench\, cloudless^{3}+\cdots\right)-cloudless
\]

Comparing coefficients of \( cloudless \), we see that \( turnpike=1 \), and for \( riverbank>1 \),
\[
2\left(turnpike\, rainstorm+footbridge\, bricklayer+\cdots+rainstorm\, turnpike\right)=strawberry+rainstorm
\]

Hence \( footbridge=1 \) and
\[
strawberry = 3\, rainstorm + 2 \sum_{sunflower=2}^{riverbank-2} courthouse\, lighthouse \text { for } riverbank>2
\]

Therefore, if \( turnpike, footbridge, \ldots, rainstorm \) are positive integers, \( strawberry \) is also a positive integer. Since \( turnpike \) and \( footbridge \) are positive integers, all the coefficients \( \left\{courthouse\right\} \) are positive integers.

descriptive_long_misleading.question: 4. Show that the coefficients \( negativeterm, nonpositive, subzeroelem, \ldots \) in the expansion \( \frac{1}{4}[1+constant-\left(1-6 constant+constant^{2}\right)^{12}] = negativeterm constant+nonpositive constant^{2}+subzeroelem constant^{3}+\cdots \) are positive integers.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Let
\[
fixedvalue=fixedvalue(constant)=\frac{1}{4}\left[1+constant-\left(1-6 constant+constant^{2}\right)^{1 / 2}\right]
\]

By the general binomial theorem \( \left(1-6 constant+constant^{2}\right)^{1 / 2} \), and hence \( fixedvalue \), can be expanded in a power series convergent for values of \( constant \) such that \( \left|constant^{2}\right|+|6 constant|<1 \). Since \( fixedvalue(0)=0 \), the series has the form
\[
fixedvalue(constant)=negativeterm constant+nonpositive constant^{2}+subzeroelem constant^{3}+\cdots
\]

Now
\[
2 fixedvalue^{2}-(1+constant) fixedvalue+constant=0
\]
so if we substitute the power series for \( fixedvalue \) in (2) we have
\[
2\left(negativeterm constant+nonpositive constant^{2}+subzeroelem constant^{3}+\cdots\right)^{2}=(1+constant)\left(negativeterm constant+nonpositive constant^{2}+subzeroelem constant^{3}+\cdots\right)-constant
\]

Comparing coefficients of \( constant \), we see that \( negativeterm=1 \), and for \( continuous>1 \),
\[
2\left(negativeterm aheadindex+nonpositive aheadtwice+\cdots+aheadindex negativeterm\right)=unchanging+aheadindex
\]

Hence \( nonpositive=1 \) and
\[
unchanging=3 aheadindex+2 \sum_{stationary=2}^{continuous-2} staticentry reverseplus \text { for } continuous>2
\]

Therefore, if \( negativeterm, nonpositive, \ldots, aheadindex \) are positive integers, \( unchanging \) is also a positive integer. Since \( negativeterm \) and \( nonpositive \) are positive integers, all the coefficients \( \left\{staticentry\right\} \) are positive integers.

garbled_string.question:
Problem:
<<<
4. Show that the coefficients \( gqnvbzay, wsjkhtup, lczmoryk, \ldots \) in the expansion \( \frac{1}{4}[1+pdlzmqha- \) \( \left.\left(1-6 pdlzmqha+pdlzmqha^{2}\right)^{12}\right]=gqnvbzay pdlzmqha+wsjkhtup pdlzmqha^{2}+lczmoryk pdlzmqha^{3}+\cdots \) are positive integers.

Solution:

Solution. Let
\[
rvnqscto=rvnqscto(pdlzmqha)=\frac{1}{4}\left[1+pdlzmqha-\left(1-6 pdlzmqha+pdlzmqha^{2}\right)^{1 / 2}\right]
\]

By the general binomial theorem \( \left(1-6 pdlzmqha+pdlzmqha^{2}\right)^{1 / 2} \), and hence \( rvnqscto \), can be expanded in a power series convergent for values of \( pdlzmqha \) such that \( \left|pdlzmqha^{2}\right|+|6 pdlzmqha| \)
\(<1 \). Since \( rvnqscto(0)=0 \), the series has the form
\[
rvnqscto(pdlzmqha)=gqnvbzay pdlzmqha+wsjkhtup pdlzmqha^{2}+lczmoryk pdlzmqha^{3}+\cdots
\]

Now
\[
2 rvnqscto^{2}-(1+pdlzmqha) rvnqscto+pdlzmqha=0
\]
so if we substitute the power series for \( rvnqscto \) in (2) we have
\[
2\left(gqnvbzay pdlzmqha+wsjkhtup pdlzmqha^{2}+lczmoryk pdlzmqha^{3}+\cdots\right)^{2}=(1+pdlzmqha)\left(gqnvbzay pdlzmqha+wsjkhtup pdlzmqha^{2}+lczmoryk pdlzmqha^{3}+\cdots\right)-pdlzmqha
\]

Comparing coefficients of \( pdlzmqha \), we see that \( gqnvbzay=1 \), and for \( kwjrmnze>1 \),
\[
2\left(gqnvbzay hugbqmet+wsjkhtup sdrkwcjl+\cdots+hugbqmet gqnvbzay\right)=ptahvude+hugbqmet
\]

Hence \( wsjkhtup=1 \) and
\[
ptahvude=3 hugbqmet+2 \sum_{zslfhdka=2}^{kwjrmnze-2} xkjgrsop mfjatlqe \text { for } kwjrmnze>2
\]

Therefore, if \( gqnvbzay, wsjkhtup, \ldots, hugbqmet \) are positive integers, \( ptahvude \) is also a positive integer. Since \( gqnvbzay \) and \( wsjkhtup \) are positive integers, all the coefficients \( \left\{xkjgrsop\right\} \) are positive integers.

kernel_variant.question:
Let
\[
Y(x)=\frac13\Bigl[(1+2x)-\sqrt{1-8x+4x^{2}}\Bigr].
\]
Because \(Y(0)=0\), it can be expanded as a power series
\[
Y(x)=b_{1}x+b_{2}x^{2}+b_{3}x^{3}+\cdots \, .
\]
Prove that every coefficient \(b_{n}\,(n\ge 1)\) is a positive integer.
kernel_variant.solution:
We proceed exactly as in the proposed argument, but make the final inequality explicit.

1.  Power-series expansion.  By the binomial theorem
   \sqrt{1-8x+4x^2}=\sum _{k\geq 0}binomial(1/2,k)(-8x+4x^2)^k,
so Y(x) has a convergent power series around x=0 and Y(0)=0, i.e.
   Y(x)=\sum _{n\geq 1}b_n x^n.

2.  Algebraic identity.  From 3Y=(1+2x)-\sqrt{1-8x+4x^2} we obtain, after squaring,
   3Y^2-2(1+2x)Y+4x=0.   (\star )

3.  Recurrence.  Substitute the series for Y into (\star ) and compare the coefficient of x^n.
   For n=1:  -2b_1+4=0  \Rightarrow   b_1=2.
   For n\geq 2:  3\sum _{i=1}^{n-1}b_i b_{n-i} -2b_n -4b_{n-1}=0,
   so
      b_n=(3/2)\sum _{i=1}^{n-1}b_i b_{n-i} -2b_{n-1},  (\dagger )

4.  Induction: integrality and positivity.
   Induction hypothesis: b_1,\ldots ,b_{n-1} are positive even integers.

   *  Evenness / integrality.  Each product b_i b_{n-i} is divisible by 4, so
        S_n:=\sum _{i=1}^{n-1}b_i b_{n-i}=4k.
      Then from (\dagger )
        b_n=(3/2)\cdot 4k-2b_{n-1}=6k-2b_{n-1},
      an even integer.

   *  Positivity.  For n\geq 3, the terms with i=1 and i=n-1 give
        S_n\geq b_1b_{n-1}+b_{n-1}b_1=4b_{n-1},
      so
        (3/2)S_n\geq 6b_{n-1},
      and from (\dagger )
        b_n=(3/2)S_n-2b_{n-1}\geq 6b_{n-1}-2b_{n-1}=4b_{n-1}>0.
      For n=2 one checks b_2=2>0.

   Thus by induction each b_n is a positive even integer.

The first few coefficients are
   b_1=2, b_2=2, b_3=8, b_4=38, b_5=212, \ldots 
All are positive integers, as required.

core_steps: Expand y(x) with the binomial theorem to get y(x)=∑ a_n x^n,  y(0)=0
Eliminate the radical to obtain an algebraic (quadratic) equation linking y and x
Match coefficients of x^n to derive a linear recurrence for a_n with integer coefficients
Apply induction (using the first two terms) to prove every a_n is a positive integer

slot1.description: Overall scalar factor multiplying the bracket in y(x)

slot2.description: Coefficients of the linear polynomial preceding the minus sign inside the bracket

slot3.description: Coefficients of the quadratic polynomial that appears under the square root
